title: Raytracing and raymarching simulations of non-euclidean geometries
speaker: Henry Segerman
abstract: I'll talk about two related projects, with two different groups, both aiming to see three-dimensional manifolds "from the inside". That is, we generate images assuming that light travels along geodesics in the geometry of the manifold. The first project, with Rémi Coulon, Sabetta Matsumoto, and Steve Trettel, uses ray-marching to generate the inside-view in all eight Thurston geometries. I'll explain the ray-marching technique, and some aspects of our implementation.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Internal viewpoint on Thurston geometries
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the goal of viewing 3-manifolds from the inside, recalls Thurston’s eight model geometries via the (G,X)-geometry framework, and surveys the visual phenomena that simulations must reproduce.

proof_stages.label: Ray tracing and ray marching as exponential-map rendering
proof_stages.summary: The speaker contrasts polygon rasterization with screen-to-world ray tracing/ray marching, emphasizing that following geodesic rays uses the exponential map and avoids inverse-exponential-map ambiguities caused by lensing and multiple geodesics.

proof_stages.label: Geometry plug-in data for simulations
proof_stages.summary: A geometry can be rendered once one supplies a computer-friendly model and isometry group, arc-length geodesics, signed distance functions, quotient fundamental domains and face pairings, lighting geodesics, and parallel transport/head-frame data.

proof_stages.label: Cohomology fractals from signed intersection counts
proof_stages.summary: The second project defines a cohomology fractal by shooting geodesics in the universal cover and counting signed intersections with a cycle or surface dual to a cohomology class up to radius r.

proof_stages.label: Boundary interpretation and Cannon–Thurston comparison
proof_stages.summary: The visual boundary patterns of cohomology fractals are related to Cannon–Thurston maps, with cusp and closed hyperbolic 3-manifold images used to compare the two kinds of objects and reveal geometric features.

proof_stages.label: Radius dependence and the sampling obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Increasing the radius first clarifies the fractal but then produces noise under one-sample rendering because nearby pixel rays decorrelate, motivating supersampling and averaging over pixels.

proof_stages.label: Limiting theorems for cohomology fractals
proof_stages.summary: The main theorems state that pointwise limits as r tends to infinity fail, normalized values within a pixel satisfy a central limit theorem, and pixel averages converge to a canonical distribution on the boundary.
